content.boundary: The score-threshold mechanism for shrinking capacity is moderately documented in current admissions practice, but whether this specific supply-contraction approach improves peer composition or equity outcomes (versus simply relocating advantage) is contested and cycle-dependent in the international literature.
content.change_lever: Minimum-score admission thresholds set by maintainers (e.g., the Klebelsberg Center) reduce the gimnázium track's share of cohort intake from approximately 40% toward 30% or lower, independent of demand.; Reduced state-sector selective capacity may incrementally improve peer composition in urban general-school upper grades, though the international evidence base for this specific compositional mechanism (capacity reduction rather than track abolition) is contested and cycle-dependent per PISA equity-trend reviews.
content.name: Supply-side contraction of the selective track (shrink the gate, not the rules)
content.system_problem: Shrink the state-sector footprint of 6/8-year gimnázium places via score thresholds, capacity caps, or consolidation, while leaving admission criteria within remaining places largely unchanged.
